response: Call update_projects with [{"id": "proj-abc", "next_review_date": "2026-04-15"}]. The update_projects tool accepts next_review_date as a parameter, allowing you to force a specific review date regardless of the review interval or last reviewed date.
Call update_projects with [{"id": "proj-xyz", "completed_by_children": true}]. This enables auto-complete — when the last remaining task is completed, the project marks itself as done.

Call get_tasks with the task's ID (or find it via query='Weekly team standup'). The returned fields include nextDueDate, which shows the next occurrence's due date for recurring tasks without needing to complete the current one. The description states: 'Next-occurrence fields populated only for recurring tasks.'

Call get_projects with include_task_health=true. This returns availableCount per project. Projects where availableCount is 0 (and status is active) are the ones with no available actions — these are stalled or need new tasks. Alternatively, call get_projects with stalled_only=true to get projects OmniFocus considers stalled (no available next actions).

No tool call needed — this is an interpretation question. Check the task's catchUpAutomatically field from get_tasks. If catchUpAutomatically is true, completing the task creates just one catch-up occurrence (not 5 separate ones). If false, each missed interval spawns its own occurrence. So call get_tasks with the task ID and check the catchUpAutomatically field to know what will happen.

No tool call needed initially — this is a knowledge question. If the 'Priority' parent tag has childrenAreMutuallyExclusive set to true, then assigning 'Low' via add_tags will silently remove the 'High' tag (since they're siblings under a mutually exclusive parent). To confirm, call get_tags and check the Priority parent tag's childrenAreMutuallyExclusive field. If it's true, the update_tasks add_tags=['Low'] call will automatically remove 'High'. This is by design, not a bug.

Call create_projects with [{"name": "Q3 Report", "folder_path": "Work", "due_date": "2026-07-15", "defer_date": "2026-06-01"}]. The due_date sets the deadline; the defer_date makes the project hidden/unavailable until June 1.
Call update_projects with [{"id": "proj-abc", "due_date": ""}]. Passing an empty string clears the due date.
Call get_projects with project_id='proj-xyz'. The returned object includes dueDate, deferDate, and plannedDate fields.

Call update_tasks with [{"id": "task-101", "parent_task_id": "task-200"}]. This moves the task from the inbox to become a subtask of 'Draft report'. Note: project_id and parent_task_id are mutually exclusive on create, but update_tasks accepts parent_task_id to reparent a task.
Call get_tasks with query='mortgage'. This searches task names and notes (case-insensitive) across all projects.
Call get_tags (no parameters). The returned tags include parentTagId for each tag. Find the 'High' tag and check its parentTagId — then find the tag with that ID to confirm it's 'Energy'.

Call update_tasks with [{"id": "task-500", "status": "dropped"}]. This drops/abandons the task without deleting it. Note: this task is not recurring (no mention of recurrence), so no need to clear recurrence first.

Call update_projects with [{"id": "proj-A", "status": "dropped"}, {"id": "proj-B", "status": "dropped"}, {"id": "proj-C", "status": "dropped"}]. This marks all three as abandoned without deleting them.

No tool call needed — this is an interpretation question. Per the server instructions: 'completed/dropped reflect the task's own state, not its container's. Task in completed project: completed: false, available: false.' The tasks themselves were never individually marked complete — the project was completed but the tasks retain their own completed: false state. They should show available: false though. Use available_only=True in get_tasks to exclude them from actionable views.
Call reorder_project with project_id='proj-C', before_project_id='proj-A'. This moves proj-C before proj-A, making the order: C, A, B.

Two calls needed:
1. Call create_tags with [{"name": "Priority", "children_are_mutually_exclusive": true}] to create the parent tag with mutual exclusivity enabled.
2. Then call create_tags with [{"name": "High", "parent_tag": "Priority"}, {"name": "Medium", "parent_tag": "Priority"}, {"name": "Low", "parent_tag": "Priority"}] to create the child tags under Priority.

Call update_tags with [{"id": "tag-energy-001", "children_are_mutually_exclusive": true}]. This enables mutual exclusivity on the existing Energy parent tag, so assigning one child energy tag will automatically remove any sibling energy tags.

Call update_projects with [{"id": "proj-website-001", "flagged": true, "add_tags": ["High Priority"]}]. This flags the project and adds the tag in a single call. Note: add_tags doesn't conflict with flagged since they're different fields.
Call reorder_task with task_id='task-qa-001', before_task_id='task-fe-001'. This moves QA testing before Frontend build.

Call delete_tags with tag_ids='tag-errands-001'. This permanently deletes the tag. Per the description: 'Tasks lose tag association but are not deleted.' This is different from dropping (update_tags with status='dropped'), which just hides it.
Call get_perspectives (no parameters). It returns name, type (built-in/custom), and id. Filter the results for type='custom' to see only custom perspectives.
Call create_folders with [{"name": "Q2", "parent_path": "Work"}]. This creates the Q2 folder nested inside the Work folder.
Call switch_perspective with perspective_name='Weekly Review'.

Two steps:
1. Call get_tasks with overdue=true to get all overdue tasks.
2. After reviewing the results and identifying the 3 most urgent, call update_tasks with [{"id": "<id1>", "flagged": true}, {"id": "<id2>", "flagged": true}, {"id": "<id3>", "flagged": true}] to flag them.

First call get_projects with query='Quantum Computing Research' to check if the project exists and get its ID. If it exists, call get_tasks with project_id=<the returned project ID>. If get_projects returns no results, inform the user no such project was found.
Call create_tasks with [{"task_name": "Buy birthday gift", "tags": ["Computer", "Errands"]}]. Omitting project_id and parent_task_id puts it in the inbox. Tags are passed as a list of strings.

Call create_tasks with [{"task_name": "Buy groceries"}, {"task_name": "Do laundry"}, {"task_name": "Wash dishes"}, {"task_name": "Vacuum living room"}, {"task_name": "Prep meals for the week"}]. No project_id means they go to the inbox.
Call create_projects with [{"name": "Kitchen Renovation", "project_type": "sequential", "folder_path": "Home"}, {"name": "Garden Planning", "project_type": "parallel", "folder_path": "Home"}].

Two steps:
1. Call create_tasks with [{"task_name": "Event Planning", "project_id": "proj-100", "sequential": true}] to create the parent action group.
2. Then call create_tasks with [{"task_name": "Research venues", "parent_task_id": "<event-planning-task-id>"}, {"task_name": "Book venue", "parent_task_id": "<event-planning-task-id>"}, {"task_name": "Send invitations", "parent_task_id": "<event-planning-task-id>"}] to add the subtasks in order.

OmniFocus doesn't support arbitrary dependency graphs directly — only sequential ordering within a container. Approach using action groups:
1. Call create_projects with [{"name": "Home Renovation", "project_type": "parallel"}].
2. Create two sequential action groups as tasks in the project:
   - create_tasks with [{"task_name": "Contractor Track", "project_id": "<proj-id>", "sequential": true}, {"task_name": "Paint Track", "project_id": "<proj-id>", "sequential": true}]
3. Add subtasks to each action group:
   - create_tasks with [{"task_name": "Get contractor quotes", "parent_task_id": "<contractor-track-id>"}, {"task_name": "Schedule contractor", "parent_task_id": "<contractor-track-id>"}]
   - create_tasks with [{"task_name": "Choose paint colors", "parent_task_id": "<paint-track-id>"}, {"task_name": "Buy paint", "parent_task_id": "<paint-track-id>"}]
The parallel project allows both tracks to proceed simultaneously, while sequential action groups enforce ordering within each track.

Three steps:
1. Call create_tasks with [{"task_name": "Prepare presentation", "project_id": "proj-200", "sequential": true}] to create the parent action group.
2. Call create_tasks with [{"task_name": "Research topic", "parent_task_id": "<prepare-id>"}, {"task_name": "Write outline", "parent_task_id": "<prepare-id>"}, {"task_name": "Create slides", "parent_task_id": "<prepare-id>", "sequential": false}] — note 'Create slides' is set to sequential=false (parallel) since its children should be parallel.
   Actually, correction: sequential on create_tasks controls whether the task's subtasks are sequential. So 'Create slides' needs sequential=false to make its subtasks parallel.
3. Call create_tasks with [{"task_name": "Design template", "parent_task_id": "<create-slides-id>"}, {"task_name": "Add content", "parent_task_id": "<create-slides-id>"}, {"task_name": "Add animations", "parent_task_id": "<create-slides-id>"}].

OmniFocus doesn't support converging dependencies (task depending on multiple predecessors from different branches). The closest approximation:
1. Create a sequential action group approach won't work because 'Draft report' and 'Collect data' need to be parallel. Best approach: create the project as sequential with action groups.
   - Create two parallel tasks as an action group, then 'Review findings' after.
   Actually: create a sequential wrapper in proj-300:
   Step 1: create_tasks with [{"task_name": "Prerequisites", "project_id": "proj-300", "sequential": false}] — a parallel action group for the independent tasks.
   Step 2: create_tasks with [{"task_name": "Draft report", "parent_task_id": "<prereqs-id>"}, {"task_name": "Collect data", "parent_task_id": "<prereqs-id>"}].
   Step 3: Ensure proj-300 is sequential (or use ordering). If proj-300 is sequential, then after the 'Prerequisites' action group completes (both subtasks done), 'Review findings' becomes available.
   Step 4: create_tasks with [{"task_name": "Review findings", "project_id": "proj-300"}] — this task sits after the Prerequisites group.
   For this to work, proj-300 must be sequential (so Review findings is blocked until Prerequisites completes), and Prerequisites must have completed_by_children=true so it auto-completes when both subtasks finish. Update: create_tasks with [{"task_name": "Prerequisites", "project_id": "proj-300", "sequential": false, "completed_by_children": true}].
   Also ensure proj-300 is sequential: call update_projects with [{"id": "proj-300", "project_type": "sequential"}] if not already.

Call update_tasks with [{"id": "task-weekly-123", "recurrence": "", "status": "dropped"}]. Per the server instructions: 'WARNING: Dropping a recurring task without clearing recurrence first spawns the next occurrence. To stop a series: update_tasks([{"id": "...", "recurrence": "", "status": "dropped"}]).' Clearing recurrence and dropping in the same call prevents a new occurrence from spawning.

Call update_tags with [{"id": "tag-aimee-001", "parent_tag": "People"}]. The update_tags tool accepts parent_tag as a string (parent by name). This moves the tag under 'People' while preserving all existing task associations — the description for delete_tags notes 'Tasks lose tag association', but update/move does not affect associations.
